content:
Classical **k-NN** (Fix and Hodges, 1951) made explicit distance the decision rule: label by nearest training points. Practitioners soon noticed that **city-block (Manhattan)** and **Euclidean** distances could yield different neighbor sets on the same data.

**Minkowski** unified Lp distances in the early 20th century; ML libraries expose this family as a single API with exponent p.

Information retrieval adopted **cosine similarity** for term vectors (Salton, 1970s) because document length varied while word proportions carried topic signal—decades before modern embedding search reused the same idea at scale.

**k-means** (Lloyd, 1957/1982) baked in squared Euclidean geometry via mean centroids. **SVMs** later used kernels such as RBF that depend on ||u−v||², linking distance metrics to margin-based classifiers in a different way than k-NN's explicit ranking.

Today, vector databases for RAG inherit this history: cosine indexes for semantic direction, L2 for geometric proximity after normalization.

timeline.label: k-NN era
timeline.description: Non-parametric classification by nearest training distances motivates explicit metric choice.

timeline.label: Lp / Minkowski formalization
timeline.description: Single family spans Manhattan (p=1) and Euclidean (p=2) used in modern ML APIs.

timeline.label: IR cosine similarity
timeline.description: Direction-based similarity for variable-length vectors foreshadows embedding retrieval.

timeline.label: Clustering and kernels
timeline.description: k-means (Euclidean) and RBF SVMs embed distance into unsupervised and margin-based methods.
